beats.text: Value at Risk compresses a portfolio's worst days into a single loss threshold tied to a chosen confidence level.

visual.sub: A quantile of the loss distribution

beats.text: Formally, VaR at level alpha is the smallest loss the portfolio will not exceed with probability alpha.
At 99 percent confidence the tail probability is one percent, so only one day in a hundred should be worse.
Assuming zero-mean normal losses, VaR collapses to a multiplier times daily volatility, the workhorse desk formula.
A two percent daily volatility book has a 99 percent one-day VaR of 4.66 percent under this formula.
The Gaussian multiplier under-states real risk because equity returns carry kurtosis well above the normal value of three.
You never have the loss distribution, so the three industry methods are three answers to where it comes from.
Historical VaR sorts past daily losses and reads off the empirical quantile, assuming no bell curve at all.
With five hundred days at 99 percent you read off position 495, leaving the five worst days as the tail.
Parametric VaR fits a Gaussian and aggregates a whole linear book through one covariance matrix.
Monte-Carlo VaR earns its cost on non-linear payoffs by simulating scenarios and repricing the book under each.
Under fat tails the historical estimate exceeds the parametric one because normality cannot see the true tail.
VaR marks the edge of the bad zone but says nothing about how deep the losses go beyond it.

visual.sub: The average loss inside the tail

beats.text: Expected Shortfall averages every loss that breaches VaR, capturing the depth that the threshold ignores.
Because it averages values all beyond the threshold, Expected Shortfall is always at least as large as VaR.

visual.note: Equality only at a single-point tail

beats.text: For a zero-mean Gaussian book the Expected Shortfall multiplier is about 2.665 versus the VaR multiplier 2.326.
Expected Shortfall is coherent and obeys subadditivity, so diversification can never increase its reported risk.
VaR can punish diversification: two independent four-percent default bonds show zero VaR alone but a hundred when merged.

concepts.title: Backtesting VaR — Kupiec & the traffic light

beats.text: A VaR number is a falsifiable prediction, so you count the days where realized loss exceeds the forecast.
Under a correct model the exception count is Binomial, with an expected value equal to the tail probability times days.
Over a 250-day regulatory year at 99 percent you expect 2.5 exceptions on average.
Kupiec's likelihood-ratio test compares the claimed rate against the observed rate and follows a chi-squared distribution.
The Basel traffic light buckets the firm into green, yellow, or red by exception count over the last 250 days.
Ten or more exceptions under a correct model has probability below 0.01 percent, so it is evidence, not bad luck.
VaR and Expected Shortfall extrapolate from a sampled distribution, blind to events that the window never contained.
Stress testing asks what happens under a specific scenario, regardless of whether the distribution thinks it likely.
Historical scenarios replay named crises like 1987, 2008, 2020, and 2022 through today's book with no probability attached.
The Fundamental Review of the Trading Book replaced 99 percent VaR with 97.5 percent stressed Expected Shortfall as capital.
The level shift is deliberate: for a normal distribution 97.5 percent ES roughly matches the old 99 percent VaR.
Expected Shortfall sets the capital while VaR exception counting still polices the model through the traffic light.
